1: Keilah (qᵉʿîlāh, modern Tell Qila) is a Judahite city in the Shephelah lowlands, approximately 8 km north of Lachish. Its identification is secure from both the LXX transliteration and the site archaeology. The Philistine raid on Keilah's threshing floors at harvest time is consistent with the ANE pattern of seasonal raids against agricultural communities — the threshing floors (gōrānôt) are the most economically vulnerable moment of the agricultural year, when the season's grain is exposed and concentrated. David's decision to defend Keilah despite his men's reluctance, and confirmed by double ephod inquiry (vv2, 4), is the first time in the narrative that the fugitive David acts in the mode of a judge — protecting the covenant people from their enemies. His subsequent withdrawal when Keilah's citizens would have handed him to Saul (vv11-12) maps the pattern of the rejected deliverer whose service is not recognized by those he rescues.
18: Jonathan's final visit to David at Horesh in the Wilderness of Ziph — the last meeting of the two covenant partners — is one of the most theologically condensed scenes in the narrative: wayyāqom yôhônātān ben šāʾûl wayyēlek ʾel dāwiḏ ḥōrᵉšāh wayyḥazzēq ʾet yāḏô bēlōhîm — 'Jonathan rose and went to David at Horesh and strengthened his hand (ḥizzaq yāḏ) in God.' The phrase ḥizzēq yāḏ bēlōhîm (to strengthen a hand in God) is a rare idiom meaning to encourage someone's faith and commitment — the covenant partner acting as the means of divine encouragement. Jonathan's final words — 'you shall be king over Israel, and I shall be next to you' (v17) — are his covenant acknowledgment of David's election. No subsequent meeting between them is recorded; the next we hear of Jonathan is his death at Jezreel (1 Samuel 31:2). This is thus the valediction of the most remarkable friendship in the OT.
2: 'David inquired of YHWH (vayyish'al david beYHWH): Should I go and attack these Philistines?' The first ephod consultation of 1 Samuel 23. The verb sha'al beYHWH (to ask/inquire of YHWH) is the technical term for oracular consultation through the priestly apparatus — specifically the urim and thummim. The binary nature of the response ('go and attack') fits the urim/thummim format: the priest presents a yes/no question and receives a divinely determined lot answer. David's practice of consistently consulting YHWH before military action is the defining contrast with Saul, who consulted the witch of Endor when the legitimate oracular apparatus was unavailable (1 Samuel 28). The responsiveness of YHWH to David's inquiries — vs. YHWH's silence to Saul — is one of the DH's key indicators of covenantal standing.
3: The soldiers' reluctance — 'here in Judah we are afraid; how much more if we go to Keilah against the armies of the Philistines?' — reflects the military reality of David's band. His men are refugees, debtors, and the socially marginal (1 Samuel 22:2), not professional soldiers. Their doubt is strategically reasonable: Keilah is a fortified city in the Shephelah, closer to Philistine territory, and defending it against a Philistine raiding force means fighting in the open lowland terrain that favored Philistine chariot-heavy forces. The men's military assessment is competent; their faith is deficient. David's second inquiry (v4) is prompted by their legitimate concern, and the doubled divine assurance functions as an oracle of divine reinforcement.
4: David 'inquired of YHWH again' (vayoseph david lish'ol beYHWH od) — the double consultation is a unique feature of 1 Samuel's ephod-divination narrative. The first inquiry (v2) received a positive answer; the second (v4) receives both a positive answer and a specific promise: 'I am giving the Philistines into your hand' (ki noten anokhi et-haplishtim beyadecha). The repeated consultation follows a principle visible throughout ANE oracular practice: significant military decisions warranted multiple confirmations (cf. the double-dream interpretation in Genesis 41:32 — 'because the thing has been established by God'). The double divine confirmation overrides the soldiers' doubt.
5: The Keilah victory demonstrates David's military capacity while simultaneously creating a political liability: he has saved a Judahite city from Philistine raiding, which should earn him loyalty, but instead makes him vulnerable — Saul knows exactly where he is. The rescue of Keilah is a microcosm of David's entire pre-kingship period: faithful covenant action (protecting Israel from Philistines) leads not to reward but to increased danger. The liberation of Keilah also establishes the pattern of David as protector of the settled population that will later justify his kingship over all Israel.
6: Abiathar's ephod is introduced as the oracular instrument available to David: 'when Abiathar son of Ahimelech fled to David at Keilah, he came down with an ephod in his hand' (ephod yarad beyado). The ephod (efod) here is the priestly garment containing the breastplate with the urim and thummim (Exodus 28:6-30), not the simple linen ephod of 1 Samuel 2:18. This ephod is the portable oracular apparatus that makes the field-campaigns of the outlaw period strategically and theologically coherent: David can inquire of YHWH wherever he goes. The mobility of the ephod reflects the pre-temple period when divine presence was not yet fixed in Jerusalem — the ark, the ephod, and the tabernacle all moved with the community.
7: Saul's intelligence report — 'God has given him into my hand, for he has imprisoned himself by entering a town with gates and bars' (niskbar ki ba el-ir daltayim uverichim) — misreads the situation. Saul interprets David's presence in a walled city as a tactical trap; actually David holds the oracular means of escape. The phrase niskbar (he has trapped/imprisoned himself) uses the root s-k-r (to shut/close), referring to the locked gates. Saul's geography is correct but his theology is inverted: he thinks the walls work against David, but the walls work against Saul too — he cannot take the city by siege quickly. Saul's repeated misreading of providential situations is a DH theme.
8: Saul 'summoned all the people to war' (vayitz'aq saul et-kol-ha'am lemilchamah lalechet keilah) — mobilizing the full levy for what amounts to a pursuit of one fugitive. This disproportionate military response reflects the depth of Saul's obsession and the degree to which the pursuit of David had displaced normal royal priorities. ANE analogy: the Mari archives document kings mobilizing full tribal levies for pursuits of rebellious vassals and bandits, but the scale was usually limited to the target's actual military threat. Saul's mobilization of 'all the people' against David's 600-man band is a sign of political dysfunction — the king is spending state resources on a personal vendetta.
9: 'David learned that Saul was plotting evil against him' — the intelligence apparatus of David's band includes informers and scouts who track Saul's movements. Chorash alav ra'ah (plotting evil against him) uses the word churash (to plow/devise, also to be silent), suggesting Saul's plotting was covert. David's response is to 'bring the ephod here' (hagisha ha-efod) — the technical command to the priest to prepare the oracular apparatus. The oracular inquiry is the immediate response to the intelligence threat: before tactical planning, divine consultation. This is the ANE model of warfare-theology: military commanders consulted oracular institutions before campaigns (Assyrian king's tamitu queries to Shamash; Hittite oracular inquiries before battle).
10: David's consultation formula — 'YHWH, God of Israel, your servant has heard that Saul is seeking to come to Keilah to destroy the city on my account (bishvili)' — frames the oracular question with full disclosure of the situation. The prayer-question format before the urim/thummim consultation identifies the specific scenario being submitted for divine adjudication. This transparency before YHWH contrasts with Saul's increasingly secret plotting; David's oracular life is public and transparent, laid before YHWH precisely because he trusts the answer. The word bishvili (on my account) shows David's moral seriousness: he is asking whether his presence is putting Keilah at risk.
11: The double question — 'will the lords of Keilah surrender me to him? Will Saul come down as your servant has heard?' (ha-yisgeruni ba'alei qeilah beyado hayerd saul ka-asher shama avdecha) — elicits two divine answers both in the affirmative. The Keilahites would surrender David; Saul would indeed come. The double question-and-answer format is unique in biblical oracular narrative: normally the urim/thummim gives a single yes/no response to a single question. The double inquiry may represent two separate lot-throws, or a more complex procedure. The crucial information is that the people David just rescued will betray him to save their city — a detail that resonates with Jesus's own experience of crowds that received miracles and then turned against him.
12: 'Will the people of Keilah surrender me and my men to Saul?' — the decisive question. YHWH's answer: 'They will surrender you.' (yisageru, they will deliver up/shut/hand over). The verb sagar (to shut/deliver up) is the same word used for extradition in international treaties — the Keilahites would deliver David as a political prisoner to the king. The city gate context of ancient treaty language makes this response profoundly political: Keilah would fulfill state obligation to the crown by surrendering a fugitive, overriding the moral obligation to the person who saved them. David's escape from Keilah before Saul arrives represents the oracular system working perfectly: foreknowledge enables avoidance.
13: David departed with 'about six hundred men' (keshesh me'ot ish) — the number has grown from the 400 at Adullam (1 Samuel 22:2). The growth of David's following during the outlaw period is historically analogous to the growth of rebel or proto-monarchical bands in other ANE contexts: the Mari documents show how Yamina tribal bands swelled around charismatic leaders; the LBA 'outlaw' groups (Hapiru) grew through accretion of the dispossessed. David's band is becoming an army, and the increasing numbers reflect the population's implicit evaluation of who the real king is.
14: David lived in the wilderness strongholds (metzudot bamidbar) and in the hill country of the wilderness of Ziph. The Judean wilderness was a wilderness of options: its topography — steep chalk ridges, deep wadis, cave systems, sparse vegetation — made it defensible but uninhabitable for large forces. Roman-era accounts and Qumran archaeology confirm the wilderness's hospitality to fugitive communities. 'Saul sought him every day but God did not give him into his hand' (velo netano elohim beyado) — the DH narrator's theological summary. The entire wilderness pursuit is framed as divine protection: every near-miss is YHWH's intervention.
15: 'David saw that Saul had come out to seek his life' — the vocabulary of pursuit (levaqesh et nafshecha, to seek your life) frames Saul's activity as a murder-hunt rather than a law-enforcement action. David 'was in the wilderness of Ziph at Horesh' — choresshah (thicket/wooded area), a place name from the root churesh (forest/wood), appropriate to the vegetation of the chalk ridges above the Dead Sea. The Wilderness of Ziph is identified with the area around Tell Zif south of Hebron.
16: Jonathan's final visit to David 'at Horesh' (vayaqam yehonatan ben-shaul vayelech el-david chorshah) is the last recorded meeting of the two covenant partners. Vayaqem (he arose and went) — the initiative is Jonathan's; he seeks out the fugitive. 'He strengthened his hand in God' (vayechazzeq et-yado baelohim) — chizzaq (to strengthen, fortify) is the word of military encouragement (Joshua 1:6-9 'be strong and courageous'); Jonathan gives David the same fortification that YHWH gave Joshua before the conquest. The human act (Jonathan's encouragement) mediates the divine strength — the covenant friend is a conduit of divine empowerment.
17: 'Do not be afraid, for Saul my father shall not find you (lo timtza'echa yad avi saul). You shall be king over Israel (umalkta al-yisrael), and I shall be second to you (va'ani ehyeh-lecha lemishneh).' Jonathan's prophetic affirmation — 'you shall be king' — spoken during David's darkest period, when he is hiding in the Judean wilderness with no army and no territory, is a remarkable act of faith and political courage. Mishneh (second-in-command) was the standard term for the crown prince's designated deputy — Jonathan is voluntarily surrendering his own claim to the succession and accepting the role of David's principal minister. This voluntary abnegation of dynastic right is unique in ANE succession narratives: normally the crown prince fought for his position.
19: The Ziphites come to Saul at Gibeah to betray David's location: 'Is David not hiding among us in the strongholds at Horesh?' — Saul had sent runners throughout Judah looking for David (1 Samuel 22:7-8), and the Ziphites respond to the political opportunity. The Ziphites' territory (Tell Zif area) was where David was hiding — their denunciation was local knowledge shared for political benefit. In the ancient world, harboring a fugitive from the king was politically dangerous; the Ziphites' betrayal is rational political self-preservation. Their repeated betrayals (v19-24; 1 Samuel 26:1) show that David's refuge in his own tribal territory was precarious — even Judahites were not uniformly loyal to their future king.
20: 'Now, O king, come down whenever your soul desires, and our part shall be to surrender him to the king's hand' (ve'alenu lesagro beyad hamelech). The Ziphites offer to perform the sagar (surrender/extradition) action that the Keilahites had also been expected to perform (v12). The formal extradition language mirrors inter-city treaty obligations: 'your soul desires' (le-kol awat nafshecha) is the treaty formula of servile deference. The Ziphite offer creates the closest Saul comes to catching David — a circumstance from which only a providential Philistine raid saves him (v27-28).
21: 'Blessed are you by YHWH (beruchim atem laYHWH), for you have had compassion on me' — Saul blesses the Ziphites in YHWH's name for their betrayal. The theological grotesquerie is intentional: a king whose covenantal standing has been withdrawn invokes the covenant God to bless informers who will help him murder the covenant God's anointed. The irony is DH-characteristic: Saul uses religious language mechanically, without the substance of relationship that gives it meaning. His blessing of the Ziphites for compassing David's death is the inverse of YHWH's protection of David — two competing claims of divine sanction.
22: 'Prepare further. Make sure of the place where his foot is (da'u na ure'u et-hamaqom asher tihyeh raglo), and who has seen him there.' Intelligence-gathering procedure: Saul asks for surveillance of David's exact movements. Maqom (place) and raglo (his foot) suggest continuous tracking of footprints and campsites in the wilderness terrain. The specific intelligence request — who has seen him there — indicates Saul is running a network of informers in the Judean wilderness. This intelligence apparatus mirrors the counter-intelligence that David himself runs (he knows Saul is plotting before Saul can execute, v9).
23: 'Return to me with reliable information (vahashivtem elai davar nachon), and I will go with you.' Nachon (confirmed, reliable, certain) is the same word used for divine oracles that are 'established/sure' — Saul applies prophetic certainty-language to his spy reports. The instruction to 'survey all the hiding places where he hides (kol-hamecharvaot asher yitchabbe sham)' shows knowledge of David's guerrilla tactics: the use of caves and rock formations as sequential temporary bases. The Judean wilderness has hundreds of such sites, making comprehensive surveillance nearly impossible without local guides.
24: David and his men were in the 'wilderness of Maon' (midbar ma'on) in the Arabah south of Jeshimon — a barren, rocky plateau south of Hebron in the Judean desert approaching the Dead Sea. The geography is precisely described: 'Saul and his men went on one side of the mountain (mippo hazeh), and David and his men were on the other side of the mountain' — a tactical standoff on opposite slopes of the same massif, with David encircled but not yet captured. The mountain that separates them is a physical expression of the providential protection of the wilderness itself.
25: Saul's pursuit — 'the rock' (hasela) in the wilderness of Maon — may be the site later called Sela-hammahlekoth (v28, 'rock of division/escape'). The narrative is building to a geographical-providential climax: David is about to be cornered when the Philistine raid intervenes. The topography serves the theology: the wilderness itself protects the fugitive. This pattern recurs in Israelite literature — the wilderness as the place where YHWH's protection is most direct, unmediated by human institutions.
26: The pursuit narrows to its closest approach: 'Saul and his men were closing in on David and his men to capture them' (vayehi saul uminashehu miqqetsath hahar mizeh vedavid uminashehu miqqetzat hahar mizeh) — both parties moving along ridges of the same massif. The syntax mirrors the geography: the two parties described in parallel clauses, converging. The word nahash (to pursue quickly) suggests urgency; David's capture is at the point of inevitable completion.
27: The Philistine raid that saves David — 'a messenger came to Saul: “Come quickly, for the Philistines have raided the land”' (chush ulechu ki pashetu hafilistim al-ha'aretz) — is the classic DH pattern of providential rescue through historical contingency. The Philistine raid is not miraculous in mechanism (it is a normal military event) but miraculous in timing. ANE historiography consistently read the providential significance of coincidental timing: an event that breaks a crisis at the exact moment of maximum danger was attributed to divine intervention regardless of natural cause. The Philistines' raid functions as YHWH's military intervention on David's behalf.
28: The place was named Sela-hammahlekoth (sela hamachlekot, rock of divisions/separations) — a name that commemorates the providential divide. Machaleket (portion/division, from chalaq, to divide/share) may mean the rock of the escape, where the pursuers and the pursued were divided. The naming of places after providential events is common in the DH (Beer of Lahairoi, Genesis 16:14; Massah and Meribah, Exodus 17:7) — topography is made to carry theological memory. Every traveler who passed Sela-hammahlekoth afterward would ask and be told the story of YHWH's protection of the anointed king.
29: David went up from there and lived in the strongholds of En Gedi — atzade ein gedi (the rocky fortresses of Ein Gedi). Ein Gedi (modern Ein Gedi, on the western shore of the Dead Sea) is one of the most dramatic locations in the Judean wilderness: a natural oasis formed by several fresh-water springs in the middle of the Dead Sea shoreline, surrounded by vertiginous chalk cliffs with numerous cave systems. The name means 'spring of the kid/young goat' (ein = spring, gedi = kid). The terrain was ideal for a guerrilla force: inaccessible to conventional armies, providing water, and offering commanding views of any approach. The David-at-En-Gedi tradition formed the background for Psalm 57 and Psalm 142 (both headed 'when he was in the cave').
